strands.title: CYCLE 85 EPISTEMIC REORIENTATION: Research Code as Trail-Mapping, Not Production Engineering; Fault Rates and Compression Asymptotes as Cartographic Markers
strands.content: TURN 255 CRITICAL REFRAME: User identifies fundamental misalignment in cycle 84 reasoning. Cycle 84 treated autoresearch loop with PRODUCTION CODE DISCIPLINE (optimization, reproducibility, controlled iteration). User clarifies: research phase is CARTOGRAPHY, not engineering. DISTINCTION: Production code: 'Code must be clean, optimized, maintainable.' Research code: 'Code must reveal the unmapped terrain. We invent the trail as we walk it. Map quality matters. Code quality does not.' TURN 255 ASSERTION: Current measurements (2% fault rates, 25x compression asymptotes) are NOT optimization targets. They are GEOLOGIC FEATURES—markers that indicate: (1) There is a phase transition or hard constraint at this point. (2) These numbers are non-obvious and counter to conventional ML system building. (3) These numbers will surprise practitioners ('you ML folks... don't know how to build systems out of transformers that work well'). CARTOGRAPHIC IMPLICATION: The research objective is NOT 'tune projection to 1% faults and 40x compression.' The objective is 'CHARACTERIZE the fault-compression frontier comprehensively enough that someone reading the map can say: "Here is where the asymptote is. Here is why. Here is what changes it."' CYCLE 84 MISTAKE: Treated measurements as CONTROL VARIABLES in autoresearch loop. Cycle 85 reveals: measurements are OBSERVABLES in an uncharted system. The fault rate is not tuned; it's MAPPED. The compression ratio is not optimized; it's CHARACTERIZED. TURN 255 CHALLENGE TO CYCLE 84 FRAMING: '25x compression asymptotes' — this phrase implies the system has a hard limit. What creates that limit? Is it: (a) Information loss (tensor can't store more without losing task context)? (b) Projector capacity (Haiku hits encoding ceiling)? (c) Eviction policy saturation (beyond some compression, fault rate explodes)? (d) Prompt granularity (current projection language can't be made denser without semantic collapse)? Cycle 84 did NOT investigate this. It proposed tuning prompt format and model choice. But if the asymptote is HARD (e.g., information-theoretic limit), tuning the prompt won't move it. If it's SOFT (e.g., prompt engineering), then tuning is revealing. CYCLE 85 POSITION: Cartographic research FIRST means: (1) Do NOT assume tuning headroom. (2) DO measure where the asymptote actually lives. (3) DO identify what creates it. (4) THEN design around it (or accept it). The 2% fault rate at 25x compression is a LANDMARK. The research question is: 'Walk the frontier from 25x to 50x to 100x compression. At what point do faults exceed 10%? 50%? Total failure? What's the shape of the curve? Is it exponential? Step function? Smooth transition?' TURN 255 REBUTTAL TO CYCLE 84 AUTORESEARCH FOCUS: Autoresearch loop (20 iterations, prompt variants) is VERTICAL DRILLING. Cartography requires LATERAL TRAVERSAL: explore the full frontier, not optimize one knob. CYCLE 85 IMPLICATION: Session 1 is no longer 'autoresearch loop + validation.' Session 1 IS FRONTIER SURVEY. Every task in session 1 should be instrumented to answer: 'How does compression change as we vary eviction policy? How does fault rate change? Where is the asymptote?'

key_claims.text: Research code is cartography, not engineering. Code quality is secondary to map quality. Fault rates and compression asymptotes are geologic features, not optimization targets.
2% fault rate at 25x compression is a landmark. The research question is: 'What is the shape of the fault-compression frontier? Where is the asymptote? What creates it?'
Cycle 84 autoresearch loop (prompt variant tuning) is vertical drilling, not lateral traversal. Cartography requires exploring the full frontier, not optimizing a single knob.

The asymptote at 25x compression may be hard (information-theoretic limit) or soft (projector/prompt limitation). Cycle 84 did not investigate root cause. Cartography must identify what creates the limit.

strands.title: CYCLE 85 CARTOGRAPHIC SESSION 1: Frontier Survey Replaces Autoresearch Tuning; Systematic Eviction Policy Variation; Asymptote Detection as Primary Observable
strands.content: CYCLE 85 SESSION 1 REDESIGN: Objective shifts from 'optimize compression via prompt tuning' to 'map the full fault-compression frontier.' INSTRUMENTATION: (1) SELECT 10-15 REPRESENTATIVE TASKS from session 1 suite. Diverse in length (20-200 turns) and type (reasoning, retrieval, summarization, dialogue). (2) FOR EACH TASK: Run with SYSTEMATICALLY VARIED EVICTION POLICIES. Define policy space: (a) AGGRESSIVE eviction: Evict oldest turns first. Low retention. High compression. Expected: 25x-40x compression, 3-5% fault rate. (b) MODERATE eviction: LRU with decay weighting. Mixed retention. Expected: 15x-25x compression, 1-2% fault rate. (c) CONSERVATIVE eviction: Recency-weighted, recent turns weighted 5x. High retention. Expected: 10x-15x compression, <0.5% fault rate. (d) INTELLIGENT eviction: Content-aware (prioritize reasoning chains, final answers). Expected: varies by task type. (3) MEASUREMENT OUTPUT (PER TASK): - Compression ratio (current untuned baseline: 22-37x, now decomposed by policy). - Fault rate (current untuned baseline: 2%, now by policy). - Task completion: pass/fail on this specific task. - Asymptote behavior: as compression pushed toward 50x, what happens to fault rate? Does it degrade linearly or exponentially? (4) FRONTIER CURVE CONSTRUCTION: Plot (compression_ratio, fault_rate) for each task. Do 10-15 tasks reveal a LANDSCAPE or just scatter? Is there a consistent curve? Do some tasks behave differently? (5) ASYMPTOTE DETECTION: Primary question: 'At what compression ratio does fault rate become unacceptable (e.g., >5%)?' Secondary: 'Is there a zone where compression improves without fault penalty? (compression sweet spot)' Tertiary: 'Does asymptote location vary by task type or policy class?' (6) ROOT-CAUSE INVESTIGATION: If asymptote is hard (information-theoretic): Analyze what context is being lost. Sample fault cases. Identify: 'This particular reasoning step requires turn #47 from 150 turns ago. Eviction policy discarded it. No prompt engineering fixes this.' If asymptote is soft (projector/prompt limitation): Analyze whether higher-capacity model (Sonnet) or different prompt format changes frontier. (7) ARTIFACT GENERATION: Create frontier_survey_session_1.md documenting: (a) Task list and eviction policy space. (b) Scatter plot or table of (task, policy, compression, fault_rate, completion). (c) Fitted curve or piecewise-linear approximation of frontier. (d) Identified asymptotes and root causes. (e) Hypothesis for why frontier is where it is. CYCLE 85 DISTINCTION FROM CYCLE 84: Cycle 84: 'Run autoresearch loop 20 times. Each iteration tunes prompt slightly. Measure 1 metric.' Cycle 85: 'Run session 1 suite with SYSTEMATIC VARIATION of one control variable (eviction policy). Measure multiple metrics. Construct full frontier. Identify asymptotes. Document root causes.' Code is messy. Infrastructure doesn't need to be pristine. What matters: every data point contributes to the map.

key_claims.text: Session 1 becomes frontier survey: systematically vary eviction policy, measure compression and fault rate across 10-15 tasks, construct landscape map.
Asymptote detection is primary observable: at what compression ratio does fault rate exceed 5%? Is the transition linear, exponential, or step-function?
Root-cause analysis: distinguish hard asymptotes (information-theoretic loss) from soft asymptotes (projector/prompt limitation). This determines whether frontier is moveable or fixed.
Frontier shape (linear vs exponential degradation) reveals system behavior. Smooth degradation suggests tunable trade-off. Cliff indicates hard constraint.

strands.title: CYCLE 85 ARTIFACT STRATEGY: Code is Scaffolding, Map is Permanent; Frontier Survey Results Drive Publication-Grade Documentation
strands.content: CYCLE 85 PRACTICAL IMPLICATION: Cycle 84 focused on code cleanliness and artifact replicability. Cycle 85 inverts: CODE IS SCAFFOLDING. The frontier_survey_session_1.md artifact IS PERMANENT. ARTIFACT-FIRST APPROACH: (1) FRONTIER_SURVEY_SESSION_1.MD (publication-quality): - Executive summary: 'System achieves 25x compression at 2% fault rate. Beyond 30x compression, fault rate grows exponentially. Asymptote is hard: losses are semantic context, not prompt representation.' - Methods: 10 tasks, 4 eviction policies, 40 data points. - Results: scatter plot, fitted curve (exponential decay fit). - Discussion: root cause (eviction policy loses task-critical context), implications (frontier is ~30x for acceptable reliability). - Appendix: task-by-task breakdown, fault case analysis. (2) CODE AS SCAFFOLDING: Run_session_1_survey.py (messy, hacky, purpose-built): - Takes task list, eviction policy variants, compresses and measures. - Outputs CSV: task_id, policy, compression_ratio, fault_rate, completion. - Code doesn't have to be elegant. It has to produce trustworthy measurements. (3) MEMORY.MD ENTRY: Add pointer to frontier_survey_session_1.md. This becomes the canonical reference for 'what is the compression-fault frontier?' (4) SESSION 2-3 CONDITIONAL ON FRONTIER: If frontier shows acceptable performance (e.g., 25x compression, <2% faults achievable): Proceed to SESSION 2 with frontier results as baseline. Question: 'Can retrieval mechanism improve beyond frontier?' If frontier shows catastrophic degradation (e.g., >10% faults at >30x): Contingency protocol activates. Design minimal retrieval mechanism. Question becomes: 'How much retrieval cost do we need to accept to stay below 2% faults?' CYCLE 85 REFRAMING OF INSTITUTIONAL MEMORY: Cycle 82-83 established artifact chain as OPERATIONAL LOG (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md tracking live system behavior). Cycle 84 extended to TUNING LOG (feedback_projection_vN.md for variant iterations). Cycle 85 clarifies: PRIMARY ARTIFACT is FRONTIER MAP (frontier_survey_session_1.md). All other artifacts (logs, tuning records) are SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION. The frontier map is what another researcher reads to understand: 'Here is the system behavior space. Here are the constraints. Here are the asymptotes. Here is what changes them.' ARTIFACT QUALITY STANDARDS (differs from code standards): - Clarity: Is every data point explained? - Completeness: Are all methods documented? - Honesty: Are failure modes and asymptotes called out? - Reproducibility: Can someone replicate the frontier survey? (Code doesn't have to be pretty, but measurements must be trustworthy.)

key_claims.text: Frontier_survey_session_1.md is the permanent artifact. Code is disposable scaffolding. Artifact quality (clarity, completeness, honesty) matters. Code beauty does not.
Frontier map becomes canonical reference for compression-fault behavior. Session 2-3 design is conditional on frontier results. Retrieval design depends on frontier asymptote location.
Asymptote location and shape (exponential vs linear vs step) determines whether retrieval is supplementary (tuning frontier) or necessary (accepting frontier limit).

strands.title: CYCLE 84 ARCHIVE (SUPERSEDED): Autoresearch Loop Framing, Composite Metric, Tuning Knob Prioritization [MARKED FOR CARTOGRAPHIC REDIRECTION]
strands.content:
[CYCLE 84 REASONING CHAIN RETAINED FOR REFERENCE BUT OPERATIONALLY SUPERSEDED BY CYCLE 85 CARTOGRAPHIC REFRAME]

Cycle 84 proposed:
- Autoresearch loop (20 iterations, prompt variants, vertical drilling).
- Composite metric (compression × (1 - fault_rate)) for single-metric optimization.
- Tuning knob prioritization (prompt > model > batch size).
- Mini-experiments on 5-10 samples to keep 5-minute cycles.
- Validation phase (turns 351-400) to confirm tuning sufficiency.

Cycle 85 REINTERPRETS these as:
- AUTORESEARCH LOOP → Redirect as lateral frontier traversal (systematic policy variation). Not optimization iteration; exploration iteration.
- COMPOSITE METRIC → Replace with frontier visualization (scatter plot + fitted curve). Single metric hides the map.
- TUNING KNOB PRIORITIZATION → Postpone. First question: 'Can tuning move the asymptote at all?' If asymptote is hard, tuning is wasted effort. If soft, THEN prioritize.
- MINI-EXPERIMENTS ON SAMPLES → Upgrade to FULL SESSION 1 SUITE with controlled variation. Every task contributes to frontier map.
- VALIDATION PHASE → Fold into frontier survey (continuous measurement, no separate validation).

ARCHIVE STATUS: Cycle 84 reasoning is not discarded; it's redirected. The infrastructure (artifact chain, measurement capability, composite thinking) remains. The direction changes: from tuning optimization to cartographic mapping.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Cycle 84 autoresearch loop design (20 iterations, prompt variant tuning, vertical drilling approach).
declared_losses.why: Cycle 85 cartographic reframing identifies this as premature optimization. The frontier must be mapped before tuning knobs are prioritized. Risk: tuning the wrong knob if asymptote is hard rather than soft.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Cycle 84 composite metric (compression × (1 - fault_rate)) as single optimization target.
declared_losses.why: Single metric hides the landscape. Cartography requires full frontier visualization (scatter plot, fitted curve). Metric was designed for autoresearch loop; frontier survey requires different instrumentation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Cycle 84 mini-experiment strategy (5-10 samples per iteration to maintain 5-minute cycle time).
declared_losses.why: Frontier survey requires full session 1 task suite with systematic policy variation. Sample size must increase to ensure asymptote is detected. Cycle time becomes project timescale, not iterative optimization timescale.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Cycle 84 distinction between 'autoresearch phase' (turns 253-300) and 'validation phase' (turns 351-400).
declared_losses.why: Frontier survey is continuous measurement, not iterative tuning followed by validation. Phase distinction dissolves. Every measurement contributes to map. No separate validation needed if instrumentation is trustworthy.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Cycle 84 assumption that autoresearch loop would find tuning headroom (5-10% metric improvement per iteration).
declared_losses.why: Cycle 85 reframes: frontier may be hard-constrained (information-theoretic). If asymptote is hard, tuning headroom doesn't exist. Cartography must first determine whether frontier is moveable.

open_questions: Is the 25x compression asymptote hard (information-theoretic loss of task-critical context) or soft (projector/prompt limitation)? Root-cause investigation via frontier survey will distinguish.
What is the exact shape of the fault-compression frontier? Linear degradation (smooth trade-off)? Exponential (accelerating cost)? Step-function (phase transition)? Frontier survey will characterize.
At what compression ratio does fault rate exceed 5% (unacceptable)? This defines the practical frontier boundary.
Does asymptote location vary by task type? Are reasoning tasks different from retrieval or summarization? Frontier survey on diverse task suite will reveal variance.
If asymptote is soft (projector/prompt limitation), which tuning knob moves it most? Frontier survey with policy variation may indicate which dimension (prompt, model, batch size) matters.
Can minimal retrieval mechanism (single-turn lookback, keyword search) improve frontier points beyond 25x compression? Session 2 conditional on session 1 frontier results.
How repeatable is the 2% fault rate at 25x compression? Session 1 will measure variance across 10-15 tasks.
What are the root-cause fault modes? Are losses due to eviction (context missing), compression (noise in projection), or both? Fault case analysis during session 1 will categorize.
instructions_for_next:
[
  "TURNS 256-280 (SESSION 1 FRONTIER SURVEY PHASE): Redesign session 1 from autoresearch loop to systematic frontier exploration.",
  "Select 10-15 representative tasks from session 1 suite. Prioritize diversity: reasoning tasks, retrieval tasks, summarization, dialogue. Range 20-200 turns.",
  "Define eviction policy space: (a) AGGRESSIVE (oldest evicted first, 25x-40x compression expected), (b) MODERATE (LRU decay, 15x-25x compression expected), (c) CONSERVATIVE (recency-weighted, 10x-15x compression expected), (d) INTELLIGENT (content-aware, varies by task).",
  "For each of 10-15 tasks, run with each of 4 eviction policies. Measure: compression_ratio, fault_rate, task_completion (pass/fail). Total: ~40-60 data points.",
  "As results accumulate, construct frontier scatter plot: (X axis: compression_ratio) × (Y axis: fault_rate). Fit curve (linear, exponential, piecewise). Identify asymptote location and shape.",
  "For tasks where faults occur, perform ROOT-CAUSE ANALYSIS: What context was evicted? Can the fault be traced to a specific turn or reasoning step that was discarded?",
  "Document all results in frontier_survey_session_1.md artifact. Include: methods, scatter plot with fitted curve, asymptote identification, root-cause analysis, hypothesis for why asymptote is where it is.",
  "Create supporting artifact: fault_case_analysis.md. Categorize faults by type (missing context, noise in projection, other). Estimate contribution of each category to total fault rate.",
  "Index both artifacts in MEMORY.md under 'Cartographic Mapping: Compression-Fault Frontier.'",
  "CHECKPOINT (around turn 280): If frontier is clear and asymptote well-characterized, prepare for SESSION 2 decision. If frontier is still unclear (insufficient data points or high variance), extend session 1 with additional task coverage.",
  "SESSION 2 CONDITIONAL DESIGN: If frontier shows acceptable performance (<2% faults achievable at >20x compression), Session 2 tests whether retrieval can improve beyond frontier. If frontier shows hard constraint or poor performance, Session 2 pivots to minimal retrieval design as load-bearing component."
]
